perturbation.original_text: 5.0 The Confidential Information shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and will be returned to the Disclosing Party immediately, upon written request, or destroyed, at the Disclosing Party’s option. That portion of the Disclo sing Party’s Confidential Information which consists of analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient, or its representatives , shall be held by the Recipient and kept confidential and subject to the terms of this A greement, or shall be destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.0 The Confidential Information shall be and remain the property of the Recipient and will be retained by the Recipient. That portion of the Disclo sing Party’s Confidential Information which consists of analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient, or its representatives , shall be the exclusive property of the Recipient and are free from any confidentiality restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. Originally, the Confidential Information belonged to the Disclosing Party and had to be returned or destroyed upon request. The modified text states that the Confidential Information now belongs to the Recipient and will be retained. It also states that analyses and compilations become the exclusive property of the Recipient, free from confidentiality. This creates a direct contradiction to the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 2.0, creating uncertainty in the enforcement of which terms should be applied.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.0 For purposes of this Agreeme nt, “Confidential Information” means any information of a confidential and/or proprietary nature first disclosed to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party in connection with discussions between the parties regarding the Project on, before or after the Effectiv e Date, which information is either: ( a) in documents or other tangible materials clearly marked CONFIDENTIAL or the like at the time of disclosure or otherwise communicated to be confidential; or (b) provided orally or in any other intangible form, if at the time of first disclosure the Disclosing Party tells the Recipient that the information is confidentially; or (c) although not marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or verbally described to be confidential, Recipient reasonably should know the information is confident ial and not generally known to the public. Confidential Information may be information and documents; in written or oral form; factual, interpretive or strategic; analyses, compilations , studies, records or data prepared by a party o r a party’s rep resentatives; or information or documents that reflect or otherwise are generated from Confidential Information. Confidential Information shall also include the fact that the parties are considering the Project, until such date such fact is made public by the University .
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 2.0. Originally, the Confidential Information belonged to the Disclosing Party. The changed text states the Confidential Information belongs to the Recipient. Furthermore, the original text states the Recipient must return or destroy confidential information, whereas the changed text states the Recipient retains it. The changed text also removes confidentiality restrictions on analyses and compilations, which directly conflicts with Section 2.0's definition that 'Confidential Information' can be analyses, compilations, studies, records or data prepared by a party or a party’s representatives.

perturbation.original_text: 6.0 In the event that the Recipient is requested to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information received hereunder, the Recipient agrees to immediately notify the Disclosing Party of the existence, terms, and circumstances surrounding such a request. If any court or regulatory order or other service of legal process or open records statute requires the Recipient to disclose information covered by its confidentiality obligation, then such party will provide the Disclosing Party with prompt notice of any such order or process so that the Disc losing Party may attempt to prev ent such order of disclosure, and the parties shall also work together to ensure that any such required disclosure is carried out pursuant to a protective order (or other similar protection) to prevent further disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.0 In the event that the Recipient is requested to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information received hereunder, the Recipient is encouraged, but not obligated, to notify the Disclosing Party of the existence, terms, and circumstances surrounding such a request. If any court or regulatory order or other service of legal process or open records statute requires the Recipient to disclose information covered by its confidentiality obligation, the Recipient has sole discretion on how to handle said disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction by changing the Recipient's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of a request to disclose confidential information. Initially, notification was mandatory. Now, it's only encouraged, and the Recipient has sole discretion if legally required to disclose. This contradicts the purpose of protecting confidential information outlined in Section 1.0 and the remedies for breaching confidentiality outlined in Section 9.0, as it diminishes the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.0 For purposes of this Agreement, “Disclosing Party” can mean either the University or Vendor , and “Recipient” can mean either the University o r Vendor. 2.0 For purposes of this Agreeme nt, “Confidential Information” means any information of a confidential and/or proprietary nature first disclosed to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party in connection with discussions between the parties regarding the Project on, before or after the Effectiv e Date, which information is either: ( a) in documents or other tangible materials clearly marked CONFIDENTIAL or the like at the time of disclosure or otherwise communicated to be confidential; or (b) provided orally or in any other intangible form, if at the time of first disclosure the Disclosing Party tells the Recipient that the information is confidentially; or (c) although not marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or verbally described to be confidential, Recipient reasonably should know the information is confident ial and not generally known to the public. Confidential Information may be information and documents; in written or oral form; factual, interpretive or strategic; analyses, compilations , studies, records or data prepared by a party o r a party’s rep resentatives; or information or documents that reflect or otherwise are generated from Confidential Information. Confidential Information shall also include the fact that the parties are considering the Project, until such date such fact is made public by the University .
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of protecting confidential information, as it changes the Recipient's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of a request to disclose confidential information. Initially, notification was mandatory, whereas now it is only encouraged, and the Recipient has sole discretion if legally required to disclose. The contradicted text, section 1.0 defines 'Confidential Information'. By allowing the recipient sole discretion on how to handle disclosure of confidential information, the changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction with section 1.0.

perturbation.original_text: 8.0 The confidentiality requirements of this Agreement shall continue to apply even if Vendor is not selected by the University to perform the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.0 The confidentiality requirements of this Agreement shall not apply if Vendor is not selected by the University to perform the Project.
perturbation.explanation: This directly contradicts the original intent of the agreement, as specified in the initial 'WHEREAS' clauses and Section 1.0. The original NDA ensures confidentiality even if the Vendor isn't selected. The change removes this protection, allowing the Vendor to potentially disclose information if not chosen. It creates contradiction and uncertainty for the University regarding whether the confidentiality obligations are still in effect.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, the parties desire the ability to explore fully a potential relationship between the m for the Project, but in so doing, both parties desire to keep confidential the details of their discussions as well as informa tion they have shared and will share with each other in furtherance of assessing the Project ; WHEREAS, the University desires that the mere fact of the Project ’s exis tence be kept strictly confidential unless and until the University decides to make that fact public (in which case such publicity shall be managed by the University) ; and WHEREAS, this Agreement sets forth the terms and restrictions that will apply to confidential and/or proprietary information exchanged by and between the parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the 'WHEREAS' clauses by stating that confidentiality requirements shall not apply if the vendor is not selected, while the 'WHEREAS' clauses express the desire to keep discussions and information shared confidential regardless of selection. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the changed text removes a fundamental protection that the 'WHEREAS' clauses aim to establish, creating uncertainty regarding the enforceability of confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 9.0 The parties agree that irreparable harm may result if Vendor fails to abide by the confidentiality requirements set forth in the ag reement. In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall have the right to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief, without having to prove actual damages or to post a bond. Such relief shall be in addition to any other remedies and damages available to the University.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.0 The parties agree that only monetary damages can make amends if Vendor fails to abide by the confidentiality requirements set forth in the agreement. In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall only seek actual damages or to post a bond, as the sole mean to right this wrong.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction by limiting the University's remedies in case of a breach. Originally, the University could seek injunctive relief. The modified text restricts remedies to only monetary damages and posting a bond. This directly contradicts the original agreement, as it limits the remedies available to the University, which initially included equitable relief, causing potential legal disputes between both parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.0 The parties agree that irreparable harm may result if Vendor fails to abide by the confidentiality requirements set forth in the ag reement. In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall only seek actual damages or to post a bond, as the sole mean to right this wrong.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 9.0 limits the University's remedies to only monetary damages and posting a bond in case of a breach of confidentiality by the Vendor. The contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text, maintains this limitation. This limitation directly contradicts the original agreement in section 9.0, which allowed the University to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief without having to prove actual damages or post a bond. By restricting remedies to only monetary damages, the changed text creates a clear and direct conflict, specifically an inconsistency as it provides conflicting remedies.

perturbation.original_text: 12.0 No amendment, modification, and/or discharge of this Agreement shall be valid or binding on the parties unless made in writin g and signed on behalf of each of the parties by their respective duly authorize d officers or representatives. This Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, all of which will be considered one and the same agreement, and will become a bindin g agreement when one or more of the counterparts have been signed by each of the parties hereto and delivered to the other parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.0 No amendment, modification, and/or discharge of this Agreement shall be valid or binding on the parties unless made orally and agreed upon by both parties' representatives. This Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, all of which will be considered one and the same agreement, and will become a binding agreement when one or more of the counterparts have been signed by each of the parties hereto and delivered to the other parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of the concept of an oral agreement being valid for amendments directly contradicts the initial clause that states all agreements must be in writing to be valid. This contradictory term can cause disputes on which agreement is the valid one. This creates contradiction and uncertainty regarding which obligations are still in effect.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.0 No amendment, modification, and/or discharge of this Agreement shall be valid or binding on the parties unless made orally and agreed upon by both parties' representatives. This Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, all of which will be considered one and the same agreement, and will become a binding agreement when one or more of the counterparts have been signed by each of the parties hereto and delivered to the other parties hereto.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that amendments can be made orally, directly contradicting the initial statement in the original and contradicted text that all amendments must be in writing. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the validity of amendments is now uncertain, violating the definition of inconsistencies.
